exemplar.title: How does the length submerged of an object change based on the density of the liquid it is submerged in?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the length submerged of an object change based on the density of the liquid it is submerged in?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables are clearly laid out with appropriate units and labels.
SI unit conversion before analysis is commendable, ensuring consistency.
Absolute and percentage uncertainties are calculated and horizontal error bars plotted.
weaknesses: Mixed units (cm vs m) and inconsistent significant figures reduce precision.
Graphs are unevenly scaled, include a confusing negative slope sign, and omit vertical error bars without quantitative justification.
Uncertainty propagation is incomplete (temperature and linearity uncertainties ignored; propagation through to gradient omitted).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data Analysis is adequate: communication is clear but not precise, and there is some consideration of uncertainties and processing. However, significant omissions and inaccuracies in uncertainty treatment and graphing limit the work to the lower half of the 3â4 band, warranting a score of 3.

strengths: The apparatus diagram is clear and supports reproducibility.
Key quantities (masses, dimensions, thermometer precision, salt increments) are provided, limiting major ambiguities.
The research question is embedded in a specific and appropriate context (scuba-diving buoyancy).
weaknesses: Research question phrasing is slightly ambiguous and could be tightened (specify object geometry and liquid range).
Notation errors (e.g., â(1/0)â instead of â1/Ïâ) reduce clarity.
Control factors (eye-level alignment, settling time) are described but not quantified, limiting full reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research Design shows strong contextualisation and a largely reproducible method. The research question context clearly meets top-band descriptors, and the procedure would allow reproduction with few clarifications. Minor imprecision in phrasing and unquantified controls prevent a full 6, but performance clearly exceeds mid-band, justifying a score of 5.

strengths: The conclusion correctly identifies the trend (length submerged decreases with density) and links it to Archimedesâ principle.
The student recognises and notes the systematic discrepancy between experimental and theoretical gradients.
weaknesses: Quantitative mismatch between fitted slope and theory is noted but not critically analysed or reconciled.
Comparison to a broader scientific context is limited; literature values for saltwater properties are not consulted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Conclusion is consistent with analysis and shows some relevant comparison to accepted scientific context, placing it in the 3â4 band. Limited quantitative justification and superficial contextualisation keep it at the lower end, meriting a score of 3.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (e.g., incomplete dissolution, temperature drift, parallax) are identified.
Realistic improvements (more repetitions, hot-plate control, suspension system) are proposed and related to identified weaknesses.
weaknesses: The impact of each weakness is not quantitatively estimated, limiting depth of evaluation.
Improvements are not fully explained in terms of how they reduce specific uncertainties (e.g., error reduction estimates).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation describes specific limitations and proposes relevant improvements, fitting the 3â4 band. However, explanations of impact and quantitative evaluation of improvements are cursory, so a score of 3 appropriately reflects the level of analysis.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and embedded in the very specific context of scuba-diving buoyancy.  The student links the question to Archimedesâ principle and to a concrete experimental situation (a jar of known mass and area floating in salt-water solutions).  This level of contextualisation is both appropriate and sufficiently detailed to meet the top-band description, although some minor imprecision in the wording of the question prevents the award of the full 6 marks.

Absolute and percentage uncertainties are calculated and horizontal error bars plotted; maximum/minimum gradients are attempted.  However, propagation is sometimes incorrect (e.g. density uncertainty dominated by volume estimate), vertical error bars are omitted, and temperature/linearity uncertainties are ignored, representing significant omissions.

The student states the principal methodological considerations (control of temperature, dissolution of salt, stabilising the jar, parallax) and shows why they matter, but the discussion remains largely descriptive; there is little explanation of *how* each factor quantitatively affects the ability to obtain relevant and sufficient data.  Hence the strand sits comfortably in the middle of the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknesses such as incomplete salt dissolution, temperature drift and parallax are identified and briefly linked to their likely influence (density error, reading error).  The impact is mentioned but not analysed in depth (no quantitative estimate), so the strand is best described, not fully explained.

The apparatus list, diagram and step-by-step procedure would allow a competent peer to reproduce the work with only a few queries (e.g. exact eye-level alignment, how long the object is allowed to settle).  Key quantities such as masses, dimensions, thermometer precision and salt increments are provided, so ambiguities are limited rather than major.

Processing includes means, conversion to SI, density calculation and three different linear fits.  Nevertheless, the theoretical gradient is mishandled by three orders of magnitude, and the student does not propagate uncertainties through to the gradient.  These constitute notable inaccuracies, placing the work in the 3-4 band but nearer its lower edge.

Improvementsâgreater repetition, more accurate hot-plate, suspension systemâare realistic and clearly related to weaknesses, but explanations of *how* each change would reduce specific uncertainties are cursory.  This meets the âdescribedâ level of 3-4 rather than the fuller âexplainedâ level.

Data tables are clearly laid out, units are present and graphs are labelled.  Precision is weaker: mixed units (cm vs m in the narrative), axes poorly scaled and a confusing negative slope sign reduce exactness.  Communication therefore meets the requirement of being clear *or* precise, but not both.

The student compares experimental gradients and intercepts with those predicted from the theoretical model, recognising a systematic discrepancy.  While this is more than superficial, the explanation remains limited and no literature values are consulted; hence the work fits a âsome relevant comparisonâ descriptor.

The conclusion (length submerged decreases as density increases) is consistent with the measured trend and is linked to Archimedesâ principle, but the quantitative mismatch with theory is merely noted, not resolved.  The link therefore is present but not fully justified.

annotations.comment: The research question is well situated within a specific context (scuba buoyancy) but the phrasing âhow does the length submerged change based on the density of the liquid?â could be tightened to remove ambiguity (e.g., specify the object geometry and liquid range).

The derivation text mistakenly refers to plotting the x-axis as â(1/0)â instead of â1/Ïâ. Clarify the notation when transforming the theoretical formula $$L=\frac{M}{A}\frac1{\rho}$$ so peers can follow your rationale.
The background theory section clearly links Archimedesâ principle to your research question and sets up the experiment logically. This solid theoretical grounding strengthens your investigation.

The hypothesis calculation for the theoretical gradient uses a non-standard expression (0.4754/0.00425Â²Ï). Show each algebraic step clearly or simplify to standard units to justify the predicted $$y=8377x$$.
Eye-level alignment, settling time and stirring rate are noted as controls but could be quantified (e.g., specify 30 s settling). Adding precise control limits enhances reproducibility.
Including a clear diagram of the apparatus is a strength; it supports method reproducibility by visually confirming dimensions and alignment.
Procedure mentions settling until âstopped bobblingâ but omits explicit timing or criteria. Define a consistent settling duration or visual threshold for equilibrium.
Switching to tap-heated water improved speed but introduces an uncontrolled variable. Report actual water temperature after transfer to verify control efficacy.
Only two repetitions per data point were collected due to time constraints. Three or more replicates would strengthen statistical reliability and reduce random error.
The raw data table presents lengths in cm, but later analysis uses SI units. Converting at source or noting unit conversion clearly prevents scaling errors.
In the densityâuncertainty table, vertical error bars are omitted in plots but vertical uncertainties are still relevant. Either include them or justify numerically why they are negligible.
Absolute uncertainty for the original mass of water is omitted, making the first density uncertainty inconsistent. Include all primary measurement uncertainties for completeness.
The table combining dependent and independent variables uses inconsistent significant figures (e.g., 0.00715 vs 0.00720). Standardize to the same decimal precision as your uncertainty allows.
The methodology for propagating uncertainties to the DV is described textually but should show an explicit example calculation for one row, improving reproducibility.
The SI unit conversion before analysis is commendable; it clearly prepares data for consistent graphing and calculations.
The first graphâs axes are unevenly scaled and the negative slope sign contradicts the observed trend. Reâscale axes uniformly and verify slope sign accuracy.
The graph description mixes units (m vs cm) and significant figures inconsistently. Maintain consistent unit notation when discussing plotted data.
Justification for omitting vertical error bars notes <1% uncertainty, but a quantitative comparison (e.g., 0.5 mm vs 10 mm) would be more convincing.
The maximum/minimum fit equations are given as ambiguous fractions (0.0001/70.6)x+â¦, lacking units and clear derivation. Present slopes and intercepts in standard form.

The theoretical vs experimental graph shows a three-order magnitude gradient mismatch that is noted but not reconciled. Discuss potential causes quantitatively (e.g. salt dissolution incomplete by X g).
Identifies a systematic error in density measurement but does not quantify its impact on gradient. Estimate how a Â±0.001 kg/dmÂ³ density error shifts the fitted line.

The conclusion restates the trend but does not reconcile why the fitted slope (14.33) differs greatly from theory (8377). A critical discussion of this discrepancy is needed to fully justify your conclusion.
Comparison to scientific context is limited to your data; include literature values for saltwater density vs submerged volume to strengthen context.
Listed improvements (repetition, different salts, hot plate) are realistic but lack explanation of how each reduces specific uncertainties. Link each suggestion to a quantifiable error source.
The limitation on hot-plate variability is described, but quantifying its temperature fluctuation (e.g., Â±2 Â°C) and resultant density change would clarify its impact on data validity.

Introducing a suspension system to reduce parallax is a good improvement; extend this by specifying a mechanism (e.g., fixed pulley) and estimating how it would decrease measurement uncertainty from 0.5 to 0.2 cm.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
